children.name: Burhinus
children.description: Thick-knees — nocturnal waders named for their prominent, swollen-looking knee joints (actually the ankle). Their large yellow eyes have excellent night vision, allowing them to hunt insects and small vertebrates under cover of darkness. By day they rely on their cryptic plumage to remain invisible against stones and dry earth. Their haunting, wailing calls at dusk are characteristic of open stony landscapes.

children.name: Burhinus oedicnemus
children.description: A strange, nocturnal wader of dry, stony farmland and steppe across Europe, North Africa, and Asia. Sandy-brown plumage provides perfect camouflage against bare ground. Its large yellow eyes and long yellow legs give it a reptilian appearance. The far-carrying, wailing 'cur-lee' call at dusk is a signature sound of Mediterranean summer.

children.name: Burhinus senegalensis
children.description: Found across West and Central Africa, often near water. Similar to the Eurasian Stone-curlew but greyer and with a more prominent white wing bar. Inhabits riverbanks, lakeshores, and sandbars. Nocturnal, resting by day in the shade of bushes.

children.name: Burhinus capensis
children.description: A widespread southern and eastern African species. Heavily spotted and streaked above, providing exceptional camouflage in dry grassland and savanna. Its call — a series of piercing whistles — is often heard at night around campsites and farms.

children.name: Burhinus bistriatus
children.description: The only thick-knee found in the Americas, ranging from southern Mexico to northern Brazil. Distinguished by the two bold white stripes above and below the eye. Inhabits dry open country, savanna, and farmland. Nocturnal and secretive.

children.name: Burhinus grallarius
children.description: Endemic to Australia. The largest Burhinus species. Grey-brown above, heavily streaked, with a distinctive white eye-stripe. Its wailing night call has earned it the name 'Willaroo' or 'Bush Curlew'. Has declined in southern Australia due to fox predation.

children.name: Burhinus alticola
children.description: Found in arid regions, the Alticola Stone-curve is a ground-dwelling bird with distinctive grey and white plumage. It breeds in rocky outcrops and grasslands.

children.name: Esacus tibetanus
children.description: The Tibetan Thick-knee is a well adapted bird to the harsh, high-altitude environment of the Tibetan Plateau. It has thick, grey feathers and a distinctive call.

children.name: Amaurornis australis
children.description: The Southern Thick-knee is a large, ground-dwelling bird with a distinctive call. It breeds in open grasslands and rocky outcrops.

children.name: Esacus
children.description: Large, heavy-bodied stone-curlews with massive, brightly coloured bills. They inhabit coastal shorelines and large rivers, where their powerful bills are used to crush hard-shelled crabs and molluscs. They are more diurnal than Burhinus thick-knees and are found from Africa through South Asia to Australia.

children.name: Esacus recurvirostris
children.description: A large, thick-knee with a distinctive heavy, upturned black-and-yellow bill, found along major rivers and coasts from Iran through the Indian subcontinent to Southeast Asia. It inhabits shingle banks, sandbars, and coastal beaches. Its large size and striking bill make it one of the most distinctive waders in its range.

children.name: Esacus magnirostris
children.description: A massive stone-curlew with a huge black-and-yellow bill, found on coastal beaches and islands from the Andaman Islands through Southeast Asia to northern and eastern Australia. It is the largest species in the family, reaching 57 cm in length. It feeds on crabs and other marine invertebrates and is threatened by disturbance on its beach nesting habitats.
